document.title: Cúram 8.1.2 Income Support Cash Assistance Program Guide

root.title: Cúram 8.1.2 Income Support Cash Assistance Program Guide
root.summary: Front matter and opening section of the Cúram 8.1.2 Income Support Cash Assistance Program Guide. It identifies the document as a business analyst reference for Cash Assistance eligibility, explaining that eligibility is determined from captured evidence and business rules for needy families with dependent children. The section outlines the main rule areas covered in the guide: non-financial requirements (citizenship/alienage, SSN, residency), assistance unit and household composition rules, dependent child and head-of-household determinations, program eligibility limits such as striker status and the 60-month federal time limit, alien sponsor deeming, resource and income tests, benefit calculation, and sanctions. Sanctions discussed include child support, immunization, minor parent living/school requirements, school attendance, voluntary quit, work registration, intentional program violation, fleeing felon, parole violation, fraud, and drug-related felony. The remaining pages are legal notices, privacy policy, trademarks, and copyright/license information.

children.title: Note
children.summary: Introductory note for the document. It directs readers to the Notices section on page 41 before using the information or the supported product. Includes the Merative US L.P. copyright statement for 2012 and 2024.

children.title: Edition
Edition — pages 5-14
children.summary: Front matter for the Cúram Income Support Cash Assistance Program Guide, covering edition/licensing info and the start of the guide’s overview. It states the guide applies to Cúram 8.1, 8.1.1, and 8.1.2, then introduces Cash Assistance eligibility rules for households based on financial and non-financial factors. Key topics begin here: program scope, required rule categories, citizenship/alienage, Social Security Number, residency, and assistance unit/household composition basics, including mandatory members and minor parent exceptions.

children.title: Edition — pages 15-24
children.summary: Covers Cash Assistance eligibility rules on household composition and special circumstances: temporary absence limits/exceptions, optional members, pregnant women, dependent child criteria, caretaker relationships, age limits, deprivation of support, and exclusion/non-household rules. Also includes child immunization, minor parent living/school attendance, voluntary quit, household disqualification, work requirements/exemptions and non-participation reasons, invalid resource transfers and period of ineligibility, and child support enforcement compliance with good-cause exceptions.

children.title: Edition — pages 25-34
children.summary: Cúram Cash Assistance rules for edition pages 25–34: deemed financial-unit membership (sponsored aliens, NPCRs, minor parents), head-of-household rules, striker ineligibility/exemptions, Federal Time Limit (60 months) with exemptions/extensions, and alien sponsor definition/deeming exceptions. Also covers resource and income eligibility calculations, deeming rules for sponsor/parent/spouse, deductions, benefit calculation, sanctions-based reductions (25%/50%), initial-month proration, and introduction to separate sanction assessment.

children.title: Edition — pages 35-40
children.summary: This section lists Cash Assistance sanction rules and penalties. It explains that sanctions can reduce the household’s benefit, make an individual ineligible, or close the case depending on prior violations. Covered compliance areas include child support enforcement, immunization, minor parent living arrangement and school attendance, dependent child school attendance, voluntary quit, work registration, intentional program violation, fleeing felon, parole violation, fraudulent information, and drug-related felony. It gives penalty levels and durations for each.

Front matter and overview for the Cúram Income Support Cash Assistance Program Guide (Cúram 8.1/8.1.1/8.1.2). Introduces Cash Assistance eligibility for needy families with dependent children, based on non-financial, household composition, resource, income, and program rules. Covers citizenship/alienage, SSN, residency, assistance unit and special household circumstances, head of household, striker and 60-month time limit, alien sponsor deeming, benefit calculations, and sanctions assessment. Sanctions include child support, immunization, minor parent, school attendance, voluntary quit, work registration, IPV, fleeing felon, parole, fraud, and drug felony penalties.

children.title: Notices
Privacy policy
children.summary: This section covers legal notices for the document: fictitious sample data disclaimer, licensing terms for sample programs, “as is” warranty and liability limits, a link to the Merative privacy policy, and a trademarks notice listing Merative, IBM, Adobe, Oracle/Java, Linux, Microsoft, and UNIX. It is useful for locating copyright, licensing, privacy, and trademark information.

children.title: Trademarks
children.summary: States the document’s trademark and legal notices: fictional example names, Merative client agreement/licensing terms, sample code “AS IS” with no warranty, privacy policy link, and trademark ownership statements for Merative, IBM, Adobe, Oracle/Java, Linux, Microsoft/Windows, and UNIX. Also notes that other company/product names may be trademarks and includes copyright years 2012, 2024.

Merative publication legal notices: permissions for personal or enterprise use, required preservation of proprietary notices, restrictions on redistribution/derivative works, and export-law compliance. States that no additional rights or licenses are granted, Merative may withdraw permissions, and the content is provided “as-is” without warranty. Also notes possible patents, no endorsement of non-Merative products or websites, and copyright © Merative US L.P. 2012, 2024.

children.title: Contents
children.summary: Table of contents for the Cúram Income Support Cash Assistance Program Guide. Lists front matter (Note, Edition) and major sections covering overview, non-financial requirements, assistance unit determination, program eligibility, alien sponsor rules, cash assistance calculations, sanctions assessment, notices, privacy policy, and trademarks, with page references for each topic.

children.title: 1 Cúram Income Support Cash Assistance Program Guide
1.1 Cúram Income Support Cash Assistance Program Guide overview
Cash Assistance overview
children.summary: Overview of the Cash Assistance program guide and its eligibility framework. Explains that Cash Assistance is for needy families with dependent children, with eligibility determined from financial and non-financial evidence assessed by business rules. Highlights key program rules: 60-month lifetime benefit limit, employment-seeking requirement, unmarried minor parent living arrangement, and paternity establishment. Notes the guide is a business analyst reference, not a technical/admin manual, and that eligibility requires satisfying non-financial and household composition requirements.

Overview of the Cúram Income Support Cash Assistance Program guide for business analysts. It explains that Cash Assistance eligibility is determined from captured financial and non-financial evidence evaluated by business rules. Key rules include a 60-month lifetime benefit limit, employment-seeking requirement, unmarried minor parent living arrangements, and paternity establishment. It also notes the guide is a reference manual, not a technical or administration guide, and that eligibility depends on non-financial requirements plus household composition rules.

children.title: 1.2 Non-financial requirements
Citizenship and alienage
Citizenship
children.summary: Defines non-financial eligibility rules for Cash Assistance, focusing on citizenship and alien status. A person qualifies as a citizen if they are a U.S. citizen, U.S. non-citizen national, or eligible alien. An eligible alien must be both a qualified alien and meet additional Cash Assistance criteria. The section begins listing 15 qualified-alien categories, including lawful permanent residence, refugee status, conditional entry, asylum, parole for at least one year, and withheld deportation.

children.title: Eligible alien
children.summary: Defines “eligible alien” for Cash Assistance: an individual must be both a qualified alien and meet the qualified alien eligibility criteria for Cash Assistance. The section sits within non-financial eligibility requirements for citizenship/alienage, alongside citizen and qualified alien definitions. Useful for determining whether an alien meets non-financial eligibility before further Cash Assistance rules.

children.title: Qualified alien
children.summary: Defines who counts as a qualified alien for Cash Assistance eligibility. It explains that non-financial requirements include citizenship, SSN, and residency, and then lists the first several qualified-alien categories such as lawful permanent residents, refugees, conditional entrants, asylees, individuals paroled for at least one year, and those with deportation withheld. This section is useful for determining whether a household member can meet alienage-related eligibility rules.

children.title: Qualified alien eligibility criteria
children.summary: Lists qualified alien categories and Cash Assistance eligibility rules. Covered groups include Cuban/Haitian entrants, Amerasian immigrants, Canadian-born American Indians, trafficking victims, certain Hmong/Highland Lao veterans and family members, and battered/extreme-cruelty aliens with specific conditions. Eligibility depends on U.S. entry date: aliens entering before Aug. 22, 1996 may qualify if otherwise eligible; those who later became qualified aliens may qualify with continuous U.S. residence. Those entering on/after Aug. 22, 1996 are barred for 5 years unless an exception applies, such as 5 years lawful residence or 40 Social Security quarters.

Defines non-financial eligibility for Cash Assistance related to citizenship and alienage. A person qualifies as a citizen if they are a U.S. citizen, U.S. non-citizen national, or eligible alien. An eligible alien must be both a qualified alien and meet Cash Assistance alien-eligibility rules. The section lists 15 qualified-alien categories, including LPRs, refugees, asylees, parolees, Cuban/Haitian entrants, Amerasian immigrants, trafficking victims, certain Hmong/Highland Lao veterans/family, and battered/extreme-cruelty aliens. It also explains the 5-year bar and exceptions.

children.title: Social Security Number
children.summary: This section states that an individual must either provide a Social Security Number or apply for one. It follows a list of eligible alien/qualified alien categories and then begins residency rules, including permanent residence and abandoned residency. Permanent residence is defined as the state where the person lives permanently, and the person must be alive to receive benefits. Abandoned residency is introduced as applying when an individual leaves the state for more than 90 consecutive days.

children.title: Residency
children.summary: Covers residency eligibility rules for benefits. Explains permanent residence as the state where a person lives permanently and requires the individual to be alive. Also introduces special residency rules for abandoned residency, migrant farm workers and their children, and concurrent out-of-state benefits. This section is useful if you need rules for determining whether someone meets state residence requirements or has lost residency after leaving the state.

Covers Cash Assistance non-financial eligibility rules: citizenship/alienage, Social Security Number, and residency. Explains who qualifies as a citizen or eligible/qualified alien, lists qualified-alien categories, and gives the 5-year ineligibility rule with exceptions. Also requires a person to provide or apply for an SSN. Residency rules define permanent residence, require the person to be alive, and introduce abandoned residency plus special rules for migrant farm workers, children, and out-of-state concurrent benefits.

children.title: 1.3 Assistance unit determination overview
Household composition
Mandatory household member
children.summary: Defines who must be included in a Cash Assistance assistance unit as mandatory household members. Covers dependent children, their natural/adoptive parents, and qualifying siblings, with income/resources counted in household size. Explains minor parent rules, including when a minor parent and their child form a unit with or without the parent’s parents, and when siblings must be included. Also outlines the NPCR or minor-parent-with-parent(s) exception criteria and notes household size/financial unit rules.

Explains household composition rules for Cash Assistance assistance units. Assistance units are built around a dependent child and may include mandatory or optional household members; an unborn child in a pregnant member’s last trimester is included. Mandatory members living in the home include the dependent child, their natural/adoptive parent(s), and qualifying blood/adoptive siblings. Covers minor parent rules, including when a minor parent and child form a unit with or without the parent’s parents, and NPCR/minor-parent exceptions that may require including the minor parent’s siblings.

children.title: Temporary absence
Temporary absence exceptions
children.summary: Explains when a household member’s temporary absence does not end Cash Assistance eligibility. Absences of 180 days or less are allowed if the person intends to return. Absences over 180 days are ineligible unless an exception applies. Two exceptions extend the allowable period with definite intent to return: care in a hospital or other public/private institution for physical or mental treatment, or participation in education or training.

Defines when a household member’s temporary absence does not break Cash Assistance eligibility. Absences of 180 days or less are allowed if the person intends to return. Absences over 180 days make the person ineligible unless there is a definite intent to return and the absence is for treatment in a hospital or other institution, or for education/training.

children.title: Optional household members
children.summary: Explains which household members may optionally be included in the Cash Assistance assistance unit: dependent children (other than the caretaker relative’s own children) and, when both parents are absent, one non-parent caretaker relative. If included, their income/resources count and they are included in household size. The surrounding text also notes how minor parents’ parents or NPCRs affect household size, and that temporary absences under 180 days may preserve eligibility, with exceptions for institutional care or education/training.

children.title: Household composition special circumstances
children.summary: Explains special household composition rules for Cash Assistance, focused on a pregnant woman in her last trimester with no dependent children other than the unborn child. Covers when the unborn child counts as a dependent child and how household size/financial unit are set based on whether the spouse lives in the home. Also defines dependent child criteria: living with a caretaker relative, meeting age limits, and being deprived of parental support/care, with related age-limit and caretaker relationship rules.

children.title: Dependent child
Relationship (caretaker relative)
Age limit for a child under 18
children.summary: Explains child eligibility age rules for Cash Assistance. A child under 18 must be single, and this rule applies through the month they turn 18. For children under 19, they must be single and enrolled full-time in secondary school, GED, or vocational/technical training, expecting to graduate by the month they turn 19; eligibility lasts until course completion, withdrawal, or the 19th birthday, whichever comes first. Also introduces deprivation of parental support and continued absence criteria.

children.title: Age limit for a child under 19
children.summary: Explains eligibility age rules for a child under 19 in Cash Assistance. A child must be under 19, single, and full-time in secondary school, GED, or vocational/technical training, with expected graduation by the 19th birthday. Eligibility continues through the last day of the month of course completion, withdrawal, or the 19th birthday, whichever comes first. The page also begins related eligibility rules on deprivation of parental support and continued absence.

Defines the caretaker-relative relationship requirement for Cash Assistance. The household must include a child living with a natural or adoptive parent(s), or with a qualifying caretaker relative. This section is part of the dependent child rules, which also require the child to be under the applicable age limit and deprived of parental support and care. Open this node for the relationship criteria that establish whether the child’s living arrangement qualifies the household.

children.title: Deprivation of support
Continued absence
children.summary: This section explains Cash Assistance eligibility rules for children, including who may qualify as a child living with a non-parent caretaker, age limits under 18 and under 19, and the requirement that a child be deprived of parental support. It outlines four deprivation reasons: death of a parent, continued absence, parental incapacity, and unemployment/underemployment of the principal wage earner. It also begins the “continued absence” criteria, listing divorce, legal separation, desertion, and incarceration as qualifying reasons.

children.title: Unemployment or underemployment
children.summary: Defines deprivation due to unemployment or underemployment for assistance eligibility. It requires a principal wage earner in the home who is unemployed or underemployed. The principal wage earner is the parent with higher income in the prior 24 months, or the one designated if earnings were equal. For an unemployed parent, unemployment must last at least 30 days before the effective date and there must be no refusal of suitable work or training without good cause. Good cause reasons include limitations, transportation, safety, wages below minimum, daycare, caregiving, illness, strike, or long commute.

Explains Cash Assistance “deprivation of support” rules for a child. A child must lack parental support due to one of four causes: death of a parent, continued absence, parental incapacity, or unemployment/underemployment of the principal wage earner. Defines continued absence as divorce, legal separation, desertion, incarceration, and notes exclusions. Also starts the unemployment/underemployment criteria, including principal wage earner definition, 30-day unemployment requirement, and good-cause exceptions for refusing work or training.

Defines dependent child eligibility for Cash Assistance. A child must live with a caretaker relative or parent, meet the age rule (under 18, or under 19 if full-time in secondary/GED/vocational training), and be deprived of parental support. Deprivation includes death, continued absence, incapacity, or unemployment/underemployment of the principal wage earner. Continued absence includes divorce, separation, desertion, incarceration, deportation, hospitalization, birth out of wedlock, and single-parent adoption, with listed exclusions.

children.title: Household determination
Non-household members
children.summary: Explains household determination for Cash Assistance, focusing on who is not included in the assistance unit. Non-household members are ineligible household members whose income/resources are not counted and who are excluded from household size, including SSI recipients, children with foster care or adoption assistance, and people concurrently eligible for another Cash Assistance product. Also begins the excluded household members list, where income/resources are counted in full but the person is still excluded from household size, such as failures to apply for an SSN, citizenship/residency issues, IPV disqualification, and fleeing felon sanction.

children.title: Excluded household members
Excluded household member exception
children.summary: Exceptions to the excluded household member rule: excluded dependent children are not counted in eligibility, household size, income, or resources; and excluded non-parent caretaker relatives are removed from the assistance unit, with their income/resources not counted and no assistance received. This section also sits amid rules on sanctions and child immunization non-compliance, including good-cause exemptions for medical or religious reasons.

children.title: Child immunization
children.summary: Each child must receive age-appropriate standard immunizations unless good cause is shown. Good cause includes a signed physician statement that medical conditions prohibit immunization or a caretaker’s signed statement that immunization conflicts with religious beliefs.

children.title: Minor parent living arrangement rules
children.summary: This section covers eligibility rules for minor parents in Cash Assistance, including required living arrangements with a parent/legal guardian/adult relative and nine good-cause exceptions, school attendance requirements for minor parents who have not finished high school/GED and the related good-cause exemptions, school attendance rules for dependent children ages 5–17, and voluntary quit rules with examples of good cause.

children.title: School attendance - minor parent
children.summary: A minor parent who has not finished high school or GED must attend school full-time unless good cause applies. Good cause includes caring for a child under 12 weeks old, caring for children under 6 when childcare is unavailable, emancipation by court, or unavailable transportation. This node also covers minor parent living arrangement rules and the good-cause exceptions for living with a parent/guardian/adult relative, plus separate rules on school attendance for dependent children and voluntary quit good cause.

children.title: School attendance - dependent child
children.summary: Children age 5 must attend school full-time until age 18 or until they graduate from secondary school or equivalent vocational/technical training. This section is brief and focused only on dependent child school attendance requirements, with no good-cause exceptions listed in the excerpt.

children.title: Voluntary quit
children.summary: This section covers eligibility rules for minor parents, school attendance, dependent child school requirements, and voluntary quit. It explains when a minor parent may live apart from a parent/guardian due to good cause, when a minor parent may be excused from school attendance, and that children age 5 must attend school until 18 or graduation. For voluntary quit, an individual generally must not quit a job without good cause; listed exceptions include quitting 60+ days before application, low-hours/low-wage jobs, other work exemptions, obtaining comparable work, or resigning at an employer’s demand.

Defines household determination rules for Cash Assistance, including non-household members, excluded household members, and exceptions. Excluded household members generally have their income/resources counted in full and are not in the household size; covered reasons include SSN, citizenship/residency, IPV, fleeing felon, parole/probation, various sanctions, school attendance, voluntary quit, and immunization noncompliance. Also outlines child immunization requirements and good-cause exemptions, minor parent living arrangement and school attendance rules with good cause, dependent child school attendance, and voluntary quit good-cause exceptions.

children.title: Household disqualification
Work requirements
children.summary: Covers Cash Assistance work rules: who must participate in the work program, who is exempt, and when the whole household is disqualified. Defines a work-eligible individual as an eligible adult (including minor head of household) or an ineligible parent living with an eligible child. Notes that work-eligible exceptions are excluded from this definition, and that certain non-participation reasons may apply. Rules are run on application and reassessment.

children.title: Invalid resource transfer
children.summary: Explains the invalid resource transfer rule for Cash Assistance. If any household member makes an invalid resource transfer, the entire household becomes ineligible for a period. Household members remain ineligible until the sanction/ineligibility period expires; applications made before expiry are denied. On application, workers must check whether an ineligibility period exists and whether it has expired, then determine whether an invalid transfer occurred.

children.title: Child support enforcement
children.summary: Explains Cash Assistance child support enforcement requirements: an individual must assign all rights to support (including child and spousal support) and cooperate with CSED to obtain support unless good cause exists. Good cause applies if cooperation could cause physical or emotional harm to the child or caretaker relative, adoption proceedings are pending, a social agency is helping decide adoption vs. keeping the child, or the child was conceived by incest or rape.

Covers Cash Assistance household-level disqualification rules: the whole household is ineligible if a member has a current third sanction for child support or work requirements, fails invalid resource transfer rules, fails/refuses work registration, or fails child support enforcement. Notes these rules are checked on application and/or reassessment. Also summarizes work requirements, exempt/work-eligible statuses and deferred non-participation reasons, invalid resource transfer determination and period of ineligibility, and child support enforcement obligations with good-cause exceptions.

children.title: Deemed members of financial unit
children.summary: Explains when household members are “deemed” part of the financial unit for Cash Assistance: sponsored aliens, non-parent caretaker relatives (NPCRs), and minor parents, including whose income/resources are counted in each case. Also covers head of household rules, how the case worker assigns and changes the selected head of household, and notes that eligibility requires the person not be a striker and to meet the Federal Time Limit.

Defines Cash Assistance household determination rules: who is excluded from the assistance unit, whose income/resources are counted, and when income/resources are deemed to a household member. Covers non-household members (e.g., SSI recipients, foster/adoption children, concurrent Cash Assistance recipients), excluded members (e.g., SSN/citizenship/residency failures, IPV, fleeing felon, sanctions, school/immunization/minor parent/voluntary quit issues), and exceptions. Also includes household-level disqualification, work registration/participation, invalid resource transfers, child support enforcement, and related good-cause rules.

children.title: Head of household determination
children.summary: Explains how Cash Assistance cases determine the head of household. An eligible unit must have a head of household, and program rules define assistance units within the household. The case worker records the household’s selected head of household and assigns it to the assistance unit where that person is an eligible member, an excluded or non-household member, or an optionally excluded member. Notes that a head of household can be end dated and replaced. Also introduces program eligibility basics, including striker status and the Federal Time Limit.

Overview of Cash Assistance assistance-unit formation. Explains that units are built around a dependent child and may include an unborn child in a pregnant member’s last trimester, with mandatory household members, optional inclusions, minor-parent/NPCR exceptions, and temporary absence rules. Also outlines dependent-child criteria, caretaker-relative relationships, pregnant-woman unit sizing, and major household determination/disqualification rules such as excluded members, work registration, child support enforcement, and invalid resource transfers.

children.title: 1.4 Program eligibility overview
Striker
children.summary: Explains Cash Assistance eligibility rules for strikers. A striker is generally ineligible unless exempt. Exempt strikers include employees unable to work because of striking workers, non-bargaining-unit employees refusing to cross a picket line due to fear of injury/death, and employees affected by a lockout. Details separate rules for a parent on strike, an NPCR on strike, and a caretaker relative on strike, including when only the striker is excluded versus when the household is ineligible.

children.title: Federal Time Limit
Time limit exemptions
Time limit extensions
children.summary: Explains federal Cash Assistance time limits and when exceptions apply. The 60-month lifetime limit applies to adults and minor heads of household, not children, and counts out-of-state months. If the limit is reached without an exemption or extension, the whole assistance unit becomes ineligible. Exempt months include some assistance received in Indian country. Extensions may be granted after 60 months for state-defined hardship or domestic violence situations.

Explains when federally funded Cash Assistance months do not count toward the 60-month limit. The exemption here is assistance received while living in Indian country where at least 50% of adults are unemployed; states must exclude these months. The section also reiterates that the 60-month limit applies to adults and minor heads of household, not children, and that if no exemption or extension applies, the entire assistance unit becomes ineligible.

Explains the federal 60-month lifetime limit on federally funded Cash Assistance for adults and minor heads of household, with children exempt. The system counts eligible months, including out-of-state months, and if the limit is reached without an exemption or extension, the whole assistance unit becomes ineligible. It also notes that months spent receiving assistance in Indian country with at least 50% adult unemployment do not count, and describes extensions for hardship or domestic violence.

Eligibility overview for Cash Assistance: a person must not be a striker and must meet the federal time limit. It defines striker status and exemptions (employees unable to work due to striking workers, non-bargaining-unit employees refusing to cross a picket line due to fear of injury/death, and lockout-affected employees). It also distinguishes outcomes for a parent on strike, an NPCR on strike, and a caretaker relative on strike, including when only the striker is excluded versus when the whole household is ineligible.

children.title: 1.5 Alien sponsor
Sponsorship deeming exceptions
children.summary: Lists exceptions to alien sponsor deeming rules. Certain categories are exempt, including refugees, parolees, asylees, Cuban/Haitian entrants, some LPRs, indigent aliens for 12 months, naturalized citizens, aliens with 40 qualifying quarters, deceased sponsors, permanent departures, Affidavit of Support cases, battered aliens, and trafficking victims. Also outlines battered alien rules: no income deeming for the first 12 months if specific battery, household, and connection criteria are met; then possible continued exemption after 12 months if additional conditions apply.

children.title: Battered alien: first 12 months
children.summary: This section explains sponsor deeming exceptions and a special battered-alien rule. It lists aliens exempt from sponsor deeming (e.g., refugees, parolees, asylees, certain LPRs, trafficking victims, naturalized citizens, 40-quarter work credit, sponsor death, permanent departure, indigent alien exemption). It then defines when a battered alien’s income is not deemed for the first 12 months: the person must be a battered refugee affected by a U.S. citizen/LPR spouse or parent, with related household members covered in some cases, a substantial connection to needing benefits, and no co-residence with the abuser.

children.title: Battered alien: after 12 months
children.summary: Explains that some sponsored aliens are exempt from deeming, including refugees, asylees, certain LPRs, trafficking victims, and battered aliens. For battered aliens, the section covers the first 12 months of income non-deeming and states that after 12 months the exemption continues only if two conditions are met. Useful for determining whether sponsor income/resources should be counted for battered alien cases beyond the initial 12-month period.

Defines alien sponsor and sponsor deeming rules for Cash Assistance. A sponsor is someone who signed an Affidavit of Support and is financially responsible for an alien unless an exception applies. Lists aliens exempt from sponsor deeming, including refugees, parolees, asylees, certain LPRs, indigent aliens for 12 months, naturalized citizens, 40-quarter workers, sponsor death, permanent departure, affidavit-of-support cases, trafficking victims, and battered aliens. Also begins battered-alien rules: first 12 months of no deeming if battery, relationship, and household criteria are met; continued exemption after 12 months is introduced.

children.title: 1.6 Cash Assistance calculations overview
Resource calculations
Resource deeming
children.summary: Explains when resources are “deemed” within a financial unit and the specific deeming rules. Covers sponsor-to-alien, major parent-to-child, and spouse-to-spouse deeming, based on household determination. Notes an exception: SSI recipient resources are not deemed. Sponsor deeming subtracts $1,500 from countable resources, using sponsor only or sponsor plus spouse if living in the home. Parent-to-child and spouse-to-spouse deem the ineligible person’s total countable resources to the eligible person.

children.title: Total countable resources
children.summary: Explains how to total countable resources for each counted individual in the financial unit. It states that for each such person, the system calculates their countable resources, then combines them for the unit total. The section also connects to resource deeming rules, including sponsor-to-alien, parent-to-child, and spouse-to-spouse deeming, with exceptions such as SSI recipients’ resources not being deemed. Useful if you need the method for computing resource totals or how deemed resources affect the count.

Explains cash assistance resource calculations: resources are classified as countable/non-countable and liquid/non-liquid, and only countable resources are used in eligibility. Household countable resources must be at or below $2,000. Details resource deeming rules for sponsors to aliens, major parents to children, and spouses to spouses, with SSI recipient resources excluded from deeming. Sponsor deeming subtracts $1,500 from the sponsor’s countable resources (or sponsor plus spouse in home) before deeming the remainder. Total countable resources are computed by summing counted individuals’ resources, including deemed resources.

children.title: Income calculations
Gross income calculations
children.summary: Explains how to calculate household gross income for Cash Assistance. It applies income rules to determine countable unearned, earned, and self-employment income, then checks against limits: gross countable income must be at or below 185% of the Standard Needs Budget, and net countable income at or below 100%. Describes the six-step gross income test for each financial unit member, including adding deemed income and combining household totals, plus separate steps for income deeming.

children.title: Income deeming
children.summary: Explains income deeming rules for Cash Assistance. Covers how deemed income is added to household countable resources/income, gross and net income tests, and the six-step gross income calculation. Details who can have income deemed and the deeming methods: sponsor to alien, parent to child, and spouse to spouse. Notes SSI recipient income is never deemed. Includes specific calculations for work allowance, unearned income, Standard of Need deductions, and support/dependent payment deductions.

children.title: Net income calculations
children.summary: Explains how to calculate net income for Cash Assistance. Apply these steps only if household gross income is at or below 185% of the monthly standard needs budget. For each employed/self-employed person, deduct the $100 work expense allowance, then 50% of remaining earned income, then eligible dependent care costs. Also deduct court-ordered child support paid outside the home. Sum members’ net income to get household net countable income, which must be at or below 100% of the monthly standard needs budget for benefit calculation.

Explains Cash Assistance income eligibility calculations. Covers how to classify countable vs. non-countable earned, unearned, and self-employment income, then test household income against two limits: gross countable income at or below 185% of the Standard Needs Budget and net countable income at or below 100%. Describes six-step gross income calculation by financial unit and separate income deeming rules, including sponsor-to-alien, parent-to-child, and spouse-to-spouse deeming. Notes SSI recipient income is never deemed.

children.title: Benefit calculation
Benefit reductions
children.summary: Explains how Cash Assistance benefits are reduced for sanctions: 25% reduction for a current first sanction for child support enforcement non-compliance or work requirements violation, and 50% reduction for a current second sanction for either issue. Also outlines initial month proration: benefits are prorated from the application day through month end using exact days, rounded down to the nearest dollar, and no payment is issued if the prorated amount is under $10. Includes a brief note on separate sanction assessment and manual sanction creation.

children.title: Initial month proration
children.summary: Explains how to prorate Cash Assistance for a household’s initial month of eligibility. Benefit is based on the application day, prorated from that day through month end using the exact number of days in the month, then rounded down to the nearest whole dollar; no payment is issued if the prorated amount is under $10. Also includes nearby context on 25% and 50% sanction-related benefit reductions.

Explains how to calculate Cash Assistance benefits after net income is determined. The section introduces a three-step benefit calculation process and starts with using the household’s monthly net countable income from the net income test. It follows the preceding income deduction rules and leads into the steps used to set the final benefit amount.

Overviews Cash Assistance eligibility and payment calculations. It explains how to classify countable/non-countable resources and income, including liquid vs. non-liquid resources, with a $2,000 household resource limit. It covers deeming rules for sponsors, major parents, and spouses, with SSI recipient resources and income excluded from deeming, and sponsor resource deeming subtracting $1,500 first. For income, it sets a 185% gross limit and 100% net limit of the Standard Needs Budget, outlines gross/net calculation steps, deductions, and introduces the three-step benefit calculation process.

children.title: 1.7 Sanctions assessment
Child support enforcement
children.summary: Explains child support enforcement sanctions within the cash assistance program. If an eligible member fails child support enforcement requirements, the household may receive a penalty based on prior sanctions for that member. A first-level sanction reduces the household payment by 25% for 1 month; a second-level sanction after a prior level 1 sanction reduces payment by 50% for 1 month. The section notes sanctions are assessed on reassessment and refers readers to assistance unit determination rules for more detail.

children.title: Immunization
children.summary: This section states that each child must receive standard age-appropriate immunizations unless good cause exists. It appears within Cash Assistance eligibility rules and is linked to child immunization guidance. The page also briefly notes related ineligibility rules for minor parent living arrangements and school non-attendance sanctions, including requirements that a minor parent live with a parent/guardian/relative and attend school or an approved program unless good cause exists.

children.title: Minor parent living arrangement
children.summary: A minor parent living arrangement sanction makes an individual ineligible for Cash Assistance benefits. The rule requires a minor parent to live with a parent, legal guardian, or other adult relative unless good cause is established. This section is relevant for determining eligibility when a household includes a minor parent and a living arrangement sanction may apply.

children.title: Minor parent school non-attendance
children.summary: A minor parent school non-attendance sanction makes an individual ineligible for Cash Assistance benefits. The section states that a minor parent who has not graduated from high school or earned a GED must be enrolled full-time and attending an elementary/secondary school, vocational/technical equivalent, or approved alternative education program, unless good cause exists. Use this node for rules on minor parent school attendance and sanction-based ineligibility.

children.title: School attendance - dependent child
children.summary: This section states that a person with a current school attendance-dependent child sanction is ineligible for Cash Assistance benefits. It also notes that children age 5 must attend school full-time until age 18 or until graduating from secondary school or equivalent vocational/technical training, and points to related school attendance rules. The page also begins adjacent topics on voluntary quit and work registration sanctions, including that these can impose ineligibility penalties based on sanction level.

children.title: Voluntary quit
children.summary: Explains voluntary quit sanctions for Cash Assistance. An individual must not quit a job unless good cause exists. Penalties depend on prior voluntary quit sanctions for that member: 1 month ineligible if no previous sanction, 6 months if a previous Level 1 sanction exists, and 12 months if a Level 2 or higher sanction exists. This page also briefly continues related sanction topics, including school attendance and work registration, but the main focus is voluntary quit penalties.

children.title: Work registration
children.summary: This section explains Cash Assistance penalties for work registration non-compliance. It states that sanctions are based on the sanction level and refers readers to work requirements rules for details. It appears alongside related eligibility penalties for school attendance-dependent child and voluntary quit sanctions, including specific ineligibility periods, but the work registration subsection itself focuses on the sanction framework and penalties for failing work registration requirements.

children.title: Intentional Program Violation
children.summary: Explains Cash Assistance penalties for an Intentional Program Violation: the individual must not be an intentional program violator, and sanctions escalate from 6 months ineligible with no prior sanction, to 12 months with a prior Level 1 sanction, to permanent ineligibility with a prior Level 2+ sanction. The page also notes related ineligibility rules for fleeing felon and parole violation statuses.

children.title: Fleeing felon
children.summary: States that an individual with a current fleeing felon sanction is ineligible for Cash Assistance benefits. The section also says an individual must not be a fleeing felon; if the sanction is current, benefits cannot be received. This appears alongside related eligibility exclusions for intentional program violation and parole violation.

children.title: Parole violation
children.summary: This section states that an individual with a current parole violation sanction is ineligible for Cash Assistance. It says the person must not be on parole and violating parole conditions. If a current parole violation sanction exists, benefits are denied.

children.title: Fraudulent information
children.summary: Cash Assistance eligibility excludes individuals with a current fraudulent information sanction or a conviction for making a fraudulent representation or statement. It also excludes individuals with a current drug-related felony sanction or a felony conviction after 23 August 1996 involving illegal drugs.

children.title: Drug-related felony
children.summary: Lists Cash Assistance eligibility exclusions for drug-related felony: an individual with a current drug-related felony sanction is ineligible for Cash Assistance benefits, and must not have a felony conviction committed after 23 August 1996 involving illegal drugs. This section repeats the ineligibility rule and is adjacent to a fraud-related sanction rule.

Covers Cash Assistance sanction assessment, separate from eligibility determination and manually created by caseworkers on reassessment. It lists sanctionable failures and penalties: child support enforcement and work requirements can reduce benefits 25% or 50%, with a third-level case closure for prior level 2+; minor parent living arrangement, minor parent school non-attendance, school attendance-dependent child, voluntary quit, intentional program violation, fleeing felon, parole violation, fraudulent information, and drug-related felony mainly cause ineligibility, with escalation rules for several sanctions.

Overview of the Cash Assistance guide for business analysts. It explains that eligibility is determined from captured financial and non-financial evidence using business rules. Key topics include a 60-month lifetime limit, employment-seeking expectations, minor-parent living arrangements, paternity establishment, non-financial rules (citizenship/alienage, SSN, residency), assistance-unit formation, striker and federal time-limit eligibility, sponsor deeming, resource/income calculations, and sanctions such as work, child support, voluntary quit, fraud, parole, fleeing felon, and drug-felony penalties.
